news_summary: Scientists and educators are advancing innovation and understanding by transforming rice into smart materials, exploring rapid plant movements, unveiling quantum time illusions, and promoting technical careers through NASA’s education initiatives.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques et les enseignants font progresser l'innovation et la connaissance en transformant le riz en matériaux intelligents, en étudiant les mouvements rapides des plantes, en dévoilant des illusions temporelles quantiques et en promouvant les carrières techniques grâce aux initiatives éducatives de la NASA.
news_summary_es: Científicos y educadores están impulsando la innovación y el conocimiento al transformar el arroz en materiales inteligentes, estudiar los movimientos rápidos de las plantas, desvelar ilusiones temporales cuánticas y promover las carreras técnicas a través de las iniciativas educativas de la NASA.

articles.title: Scientists discover a strange property in rice and turn it into a smart material
articles.summary: Scientists discovered that rice behaves in a highly unusual way: it weakens under rapid compression but stays stronger when pressure is applied slowly. Using this effect, they engineered a new material that reacts differently to gentle movements and sudden impacts. The material can adapt its stiffness automatically, opening the door to safer soft robots and protective equipment that responds instantly to collisions.
articles.content:
Rice is best known as one of the world's most important food crops, but scientists have now shown it could also help inspire a new generation of smart materials.

Researchers discovered that packed rice grains behave in an unusual way under pressure. When compressed slowly, the grains remain relatively strong. But when squeezed quickly, they actually become weaker. This surprising behavior has allowed scientists to create a new material that could one day be used in soft robots that automatically adjust their stiffness and protective equipment that responds differently depending on the force of an impact.

The international research team, led by the University of Birmingham, reported its findings in the journal Matter.

Rice's Unusual Response to Pressure

Experiments showed that tightly packed rice grains respond very differently depending on how quickly a load is applied. At higher loading speeds, the material weakens significantly.

This phenomenon, known as "rate softening," is uncommon in most materials. Researchers found that it happens because friction between individual rice grains drops sharply when forces are applied rapidly. As a result, the internal networks of forces that normally help support the load become weaker.

The team used this unusual property to develop a new metamaterial, an engineered composite structure designed to exhibit behaviors not found in naturally occurring materials.

Creating a Self-Adapting Metamaterial

To build the new material, researchers combined rice-based granular units with materials such as sand, which become stronger when subjected to rapid loading. The result was a granular metamaterial capable of responding differently to slow movements and sudden impacts.

Depending on the situation, the material can bend, buckle, or stiffen in different ways, all without electronics, sensors, or active control systems.

Dr. Mingchao Liu, from the University of Birmingham, said: "Rice might be best known as a staple food globally, but it's rarely associated with advanced engineering. Our research shows that it can form the basis of a new class of functional materials.

"Rather than treating this phenomenon as curiosity, we turned it into a design principle. This approach enabled us to create a material that can bend, buckle, or stiffen differently under slow movements versus sudden impacts -- without electronics, sensors, or active control. Instead of telling a structure how to respond, we let physics decide: fast loads trigger one behavior, slow loads another."

The researchers say the work highlights how common granular materials can be transformed into engineered systems that respond intelligently through their own mechanical properties.

Potential Applications in Robotics and Safety Gear

The speed-sensitive metamaterial could open new possibilities in soft robotics. Unlike traditional metal robots, future systems built with these materials could be lighter, safer, and more adaptable.

Such robots could be especially useful for working alongside people, operating in challenging environments, and performing delicate tasks, including assisting with surgery.

The material may also have applications in protective equipment. Because it can respond differently depending on the speed of an impact, it could absorb energy or deform in a controlled way during a collision, helping reduce the risk of injury.

Importantly, these responses occur without the need for electronics, external power, or sensors, allowing the material itself to automatically adapt to changing conditions.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about rice's unusual mechanical properties leading to the creation of a novel self-adapting metamaterial. This material has broad potential applications in soft robotics and protective equipment, which could enhance safety and adaptability without relying on electronics or sensors. The discovery represents a meaningful technological breakthrough with tangible benefits for society.
articles.personality_title: Scientists turn rice’s unique pressure behavior into smart material for safer robots and gear
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Rice is a common food worldwide, but scientists have found it shows a surprising behavior when pressure is applied. This discovery led to creating a new kind of material that can change its stiffness on its own.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Birmingham and their international team tested packed rice grains and found they get weaker when squeezed quickly but stay strong under slow pressure. They used this “rate softening” effect to build a special material, mixing rice grains with sand. This new material can bend or stiffen differently depending on how fast it is pressed, without needing electronics or sensors.

**Impact** – This smart material can improve soft robots, making them lighter and safer by automatically adjusting stiffness based on movement speed. It can also be used in protective gear that reacts instantly to impacts, helping reduce injuries. The material’s ability to adapt without extra parts means it could be simpler and more reliable than current technologies.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will likely work on refining this material and testing it in real-world uses, such as in robots that assist people or in safety equipment like helmets or pads. This could lead to new products that protect better and work more smoothly in everyday life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By using rice’s unusual reaction to pressure, scientists created a smart material that changes stiffness on its own, offering new ways to build safer robots and protective gear.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques transforment le comportement unique du riz sous pression en matériau intelligent pour robots et équipements plus sûrs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le riz est un aliment courant dans le monde, mais les scientifiques ont découvert qu’il réagit de façon surprenante sous pression. Cette découverte a permis de créer un nouveau type de matériau capable de changer de rigidité tout seul.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Birmingham et une équipe internationale ont testé des grains de riz compactés et ont constaté qu’ils deviennent plus faibles lorsqu’ils sont pressés rapidement, mais restent solides sous une pression lente. Ils ont utilisé cet effet appelé « ramollissement en fonction de la vitesse » pour fabriquer un matériau spécial, en mélangeant le riz avec du sable. Ce nouveau matériau peut se plier ou se rigidifier différemment selon la vitesse de la pression, sans besoin d’électronique ni de capteurs.

**Impact** – Ce matériau intelligent peut améliorer les robots souples, les rendant plus légers et plus sûrs en ajustant automatiquement leur rigidité selon le mouvement. Il peut aussi être utilisé dans des équipements de protection qui réagissent instantanément aux chocs, aidant à réduire les blessures. Sa capacité à s’adapter sans pièces supplémentaires le rend plus simple et fiable que les technologies actuelles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement affiner ce matériau et le tester dans des usages concrets, comme dans des robots d’assistance ou des équipements de sécurité tels que casques ou protections. Cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux produits offrant plus de protection et un fonctionnement plus fluide au quotidien.

**Une phrase clé à retenir** – En utilisant la réaction unique du riz à la pression, les scientifiques ont créé un matériau intelligent qui change de rigidité tout seul, ouvrant la voie à des robots et équipements de protection plus sûrs.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos convierten el comportamiento único del arroz bajo presión en un material inteligente para robots y equipo más seguros
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El arroz es un alimento común en todo el mundo, pero los científicos descubrieron que se comporta de manera sorprendente cuando se le aplica presión. Este hallazgo llevó a crear un nuevo tipo de material que puede cambiar su dureza por sí solo.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Birmingham y un equipo internacional probaron granos de arroz compactados y encontraron que se debilitan cuando se presionan rápidamente, pero se mantienen fuertes bajo presión lenta. Usaron este efecto llamado “ablandamiento por velocidad” para fabricar un material especial, mezclando arroz con arena. Este nuevo material puede doblarse o endurecerse de forma diferente según la rapidez con que se aplique la presión, sin necesitar electrónica ni sensores.

**Impacto** – Este material inteligente puede mejorar los robots blandos, haciéndolos más ligeros y seguros al ajustar automáticamente su dureza según el movimiento. También puede usarse en equipos de protección que reaccionan al instante a los impactos, ayudando a reducir lesiones. Su capacidad para adaptarse sin partes adicionales lo hace más simple y confiable que las tecnologías actuales.

**Próximo paso** – Lo más probable es que los científicos perfeccionen este material y lo prueben en usos reales, como en robots que asisten a personas o en equipo de seguridad como cascos o protectores. Esto podría llevar a nuevos productos que protejan mejor y funcionen mejor en la vida diaria.

**Una frase para recordar** – Usando la reacción única del arroz a la presión, los científicos crearon un material inteligente que cambia su dureza por sí solo, abriendo nuevas formas de fabricar robots y equipos de protección más seguros.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of tightly packed rice grains and sand particles interlocking like puzzle pieces, with subtle visual cues of gentle compression on one side and sudden impact on the other, showing the grains softly bending and buckling under slow pressure while appearing more dispersed and weakened under fast force, all rendered in natural beige, soft browns, and muted yellows against a simple, light background.

articles.title: NASA’s Career Technical Education Day Highlights Technical Careers
articles.summary: At NASA, remaining a global leader in exploration and innovation includes having a skilledand dedicated workforce. Technicians play a critical role in advancing the agency’sresearch and missions, applying hands-on expertise across engineering, fabrication,electronics, and countless other technical fields. To help cultivate the next generation of technical talent, NASA’s Office of STEM Engagementhosted Career Technical Education […]
articles.content:
Students participate in a hands-on robotics demonstration during Career Technical Education Day at NASA’s Langley Research Center in Hampton, Virginia. NASA/Mark Knopp

At NASA, remaining a global leader in exploration and innovation includes having a skilled

and dedicated workforce. Technicians play a critical role in advancing the agency’s

research and missions, applying hands-on expertise across engineering, fabrication,

electronics, and countless other technical fields.

To help cultivate the next generation of technical talent, NASA’s Office of STEM Engagement

hosted Career Technical Education Day recently at NASA’s Langley Research Center in

Hampton, Virginia. One hundred high school and community college students from Virginia

and North Carolina attended, eager to explore the technical career paths that help drive

NASA’s work.

“Many students picture NASA as only astronauts or engineers and therefore never consider

a career at NASA to be within their reach,” said Bonnie Murray, lead for the Office of STEM

Engagement at NASA Langley. “Bringing students from local career and technical

education programs to Langley allows them the opportunity to see technicians at work,

hear the pathways those technicians followed, and understand how the skills they are

developing in their related classes have a place in the NASA workforce.”

The event opened with remarks from NASA Langley’s Steve Gayle, who traced his path from

an engineering technician co-op in the center’s Fabrication Division and a graduate of

Langley’s Engineering Technician Apprentice Program to his current role as acting

associate director. Gayle encouraged students to embrace challenges, think critically, stay

curious, and create their own opportunities as they pursue their career goals.

“We need young, bright minds,” Gayle said. “At NASA, we rely on skilled hands-on

professionals — technicians who operate our wind tunnels, apply their skills in our

fabrication shops, and use their electronics knowledge to design, test, and build critical

systems.”

Students visit NASA Langley Research Center’s model shop during Career Technical Education Day to learn about the materials and techniques technicians use to build model aircraft and spacecraft. NASA/Ryan Hill

Throughout the day, students toured several of Langley’s world-class facilities, including

the historic Landing and Impact Research Facility and one of the center’s wind tunnels. At

each stop, they received a behind-the-scenes look at the spaces where NASA technicians

build, test, and refine the tools and technologies that support the agency’s missions. The

technicians spoke with students about their work, their career paths, and the skills needed

to excel in technical roles.

Hands-on demonstrations and interactive activities lead by NASA technicians and

aerospace industry partners helped students connect their classroom experience with

real-world applications. Whether observing fabrication techniques, seeing instrumentation

up close, or engaging with engineering demonstrations, participants experienced how

STEM and technical skills directly translate into meaningful careers.

“Through events such as this, NASA seeks to prepare students for aerospace careers

through experiences and investments that strengthen research capacity, build technical

expertise, and expand reach in alignment with agency missions and needs,” Murray said.

The event ended with a career panel moderated by NASA astronaut Joe Acaba, associate

director of mission and strategy at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston and former

math and science teacher. The panel featured four Langley technician apprentices who

shared insights into their roles and the value of strong foundational skills in technical

fields.

Wyatt Healy, mechanical engineering technician apprentice at NASA’s Langley Research Center, answers questions during a career panel featuring NASA Langley technician apprentices during Career Technical Education Day. NASA/Ryan Hill

“A basic grasp of how software, systems, and even everyday items function goes a long way

as you progress in your technician journey,” said Wyatt Healy, mechanical engineering

technician apprentice at NASA Langley. “When you have those fundamentals down,

learning the more advanced concepts becomes much easier. It doesn’t happen overnight,

but with a strong foundation, the sky is the limit.”

By connecting students with NASA professionals, facilities, and hands-on experiences, the

event showcased a broad range of opportunities available in technical careers. It also

underscored NASA’s commitment to building a strong, skilled workforce equipped to

support the agency’s mission and tackle the challenges of tomorrow.

For more information about opportunities to connect students with NASA’s mission, work, and people, visit:

https://www.nasa.gov/learning-resources

Brittny McGraw

NASA Langley Research Center
articles.reasoning: The article describes a NASA initiative to inspire and cultivate the next generation of technical talent through hands-on education and career exposure, which has broad positive implications for workforce development in STEM fields and supports NASA's mission. It focuses on a specific, impactful event with detailed context and demonstrates tangible benefits for students and society.
articles.personality_title: NASA hosts Career Technical Education Day to inspire future technicians
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA needs skilled technicians to support its space missions and research. These workers use hands-on skills in engineering, electronics, and fabrication to build and test important technology.

**What happened** – NASA’s Office of STEM Engagement held Career Technical Education Day at the Langley Research Center in Virginia. One hundred high school and community college students from Virginia and North Carolina visited to learn about technical careers at NASA. They toured facilities, watched demonstrations, and met technicians who shared their career journeys.

**Impact** – The event helped students see that NASA jobs go beyond astronauts and engineers. It showed them how their classroom skills can lead to real technical careers at NASA. Meeting current technicians and exploring NASA’s labs gave students a clear picture of possible futures in science and technology fields.

**What's next step** – NASA plans to continue hosting events like this to connect more students with technical career pathways. These efforts support NASA’s goal of building a strong workforce ready to meet future challenges in space exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Career Technical Education Day gives students hands-on experience and guidance to pursue important technical careers that support space missions.
articles.personality_title_fr: La journée d’éducation technique de la NASA inspire les futurs techniciens
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a besoin de techniciens qualifiés pour soutenir ses missions spatiales et ses recherches. Ces professionnels utilisent des compétences pratiques en ingénierie, électronique et fabrication pour construire et tester des technologies importantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le bureau STEM de la NASA a organisé une journée d’éducation technique au centre de recherche Langley en Virginie. Cent élèves de lycées et collèges communautaires de Virginie et de Caroline du Nord ont visité le centre pour découvrir les carrières techniques à la NASA. Ils ont fait des visites, assisté à des démonstrations et rencontré des techniciens qui ont partagé leur parcours professionnel.

**Impact** – L’événement a permis aux élèves de comprendre que les emplois à la NASA ne sont pas seulement pour les astronautes ou ingénieurs. Ils ont vu comment leurs compétences scolaires peuvent conduire à de vraies carrières techniques. Rencontrer les techniciens et visiter les laboratoires a donné aux élèves une image claire de leurs futures possibilités dans les sciences et la technologie.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA prévoit de continuer à organiser ce type d’événements pour connecter davantage d’élèves aux carrières techniques. Ces initiatives soutiennent l’objectif de la NASA de construire une main-d’œuvre solide pour relever les futurs défis de l’exploration spatiale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La journée d’éducation technique de la NASA offre aux élèves une expérience pratique et des conseils pour suivre des carrières techniques essentielles aux missions spatiales.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA organiza el Día de Educación Técnica para inspirar a futuros técnicos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA necesita técnicos capacitados para apoyar sus misiones espaciales e investigaciones. Estos trabajadores usan habilidades prácticas en ingeniería, electrónica y fabricación para construir y probar tecnología importante.

**Qué pasó** – La Oficina de Participación STEM de la NASA organizó el Día de Educación Técnica en el Centro de Investigación Langley en Virginia. Cien estudiantes de secundaria y colegios comunitarios de Virginia y Carolina del Norte visitaron para conocer las carreras técnicas en la NASA. Recibieron tours, vieron demostraciones y hablaron con técnicos que compartieron sus experiencias.

**Impacto** – El evento ayudó a los estudiantes a entender que los trabajos en la NASA no son solo para astronautas o ingenieros. Les mostró cómo sus habilidades escolares pueden llevar a carreras técnicas reales. Conocer a los técnicos y explorar los laboratorios les dio una idea clara de las oportunidades futuras en ciencia y tecnología.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA planea seguir organizando eventos como este para conectar a más estudiantes con carreras técnicas. Estas acciones apoyan la meta de la NASA de formar una fuerza laboral fuerte para enfrentar los desafíos futuros de la exploración espacial.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Día de Educación Técnica de la NASA brinda a los estudiantes experiencia práctica y orientación para seguir carreras técnicas que apoyan las misiones espaciales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a group of diverse young silhouettes gathered around a large, intricate robotic arm and mechanical tools, set inside a softly lit workshop filled with model aircraft and spacecraft parts, with subtle representations of wind tunnels and fabrication equipment in the background, symbolizing hands-on technical learning and career exploration at NASA’s Langley Research Center.

articles.title: Revealed: how Venus flytraps snap shut with astonishing speed
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 11 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01857-2Softening of the cells on the outermost surface of the trap lets the plant move at a breakneck pace.
articles.content:
The lobes that form the Venus flytrap’s mouth curve inward to trap prey.Credit: Chris Mattison/Nature Picture Library

Ever since renowned biologist Charles Darwin proclaimed in 1875 that the carnivorous Venus flytrap was one of the “most wonderful” plants in the world, scientists have been trying to work out how it snaps shut so quickly on its prey. Plants are not known for their speed, but the flytrap (Dionaea muscipula) closes in less than a second.

A research team has now snapped a key piece of the puzzle in place. Experiments showed that, after an insect crawls into the flytrap’s maw, cells on the outer surface of the hinged leaf that forms the plant’s ‘mouth’ soften. This allows the leaf to change shape and hinge shut, the researchers report today in Science1.

Little lab of horrors

“This is a breathtaking, very elegant paper,” says biomechanics researcher Simon Poppinga, director of the botanical garden at the Technical University of Darmstadt in Germany. Plants can relax the rigid outer walls of their cells to enable growth, but that process happens over a much slower timescale than the flytrap’s snap, says Poppinga, who was not involved in the study. Cell softening at the pace seen in the flytrap is a phenomenon scientists haven’t seen before, he says.

The discovery could help scientists to better understand basic plant processes, he adds, and it could inspire them to design sophisticated soft robots that spring into action when one of their materials changes its stiffness.

What a softie

Ever since Darwin admired the “rapidity” of the Venus flytrap, researchers have been solving its mysteries one by one. Some studies have shown that when insects stimulate ‘hairs’ in the plant’s mouth, an electrical impulse runs across the leaf, triggering it to imprison and digest its prey2. In 2005, physicist Yoël Forterre, at what has since become Aix-Marseille University in France, and his colleagues reported that, when the hinged leaf forming the plant’s mouth is in the ‘open’ position, its two lobes are curved outwards in a strained configuration. The team found that, when an insect arrives, the strain is released suddenly — flipping the lobes to curve inward and closing the trap in as little as one-tenth of a second3.

How plants evolved into carnivores

Twenty-one years later, Forterre has solved another mystery. Researchers have long debated how the strain in the flytrap’s leaves is released. One theory suggests that water moves rapidly from the inner surface of the trap to epidermal cells on its outer surface, leading to swelling that drives the transition. Another theory holds that the rigid walls of the outer epidermal cells soften suddenly, releasing the strain.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery explaining the rapid movement mechanism of the Venus flytrap, revealing a novel biological process of cell softening at unprecedented speed. This breakthrough advances fundamental plant biology knowledge and has potential applications in designing advanced soft robotics, which could benefit technology and engineering fields broadly.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover how Venus flytraps close their traps so fast
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Venus flytrap is a carnivorous plant famous for snapping its leaf traps shut very quickly to catch insects. Scientists have studied this plant for many years, trying to understand how it moves so fast when most plants move slowly.

**What happened** – A recent study found that the outer cells of the flytrap’s leaf soften quickly after an insect touches it. This softening allows the leaf to bend inward and close the trap in less than a second. This discovery was reported in the journal Science in June 2026.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have seen plant cells soften so fast. Understanding this process helps us learn more about how plants work. It also could inspire engineers to create soft robots that change shape quickly by changing how stiff their materials are.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will likely explore how this fast cell softening happens at a detailed level. They may also try to use this idea to build new types of robots or devices that move quickly and smoothly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered that quick softening of outer cells lets Venus flytraps snap shut rapidly, revealing a new plant movement process with possible uses in soft robotics.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent comment les pièges de la dionée se referment si rapidement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La dionée attrape-mouche est une plante carnivore célèbre pour refermer ses feuilles pièges très rapidement afin de capturer des insectes. Les scientifiques étudient cette plante depuis longtemps pour comprendre comment elle bouge si vite alors que la plupart des plantes bougent lentement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude récente a montré que les cellules extérieures de la feuille de la dionée s’assouplissent rapidement après qu’un insecte la touche. Cet assouplissement permet à la feuille de se plier vers l’intérieur et de refermer le piège en moins d’une seconde. Cette découverte a été publiée dans la revue Science en juin 2026.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques observent un assouplissement aussi rapide des cellules végétales. Comprendre ce processus aide à mieux connaître le fonctionnement des plantes. Cela pourrait aussi inspirer les ingénieurs à créer des robots souples capables de changer de forme rapidement en modifiant la rigidité de leurs matériaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement étudier en détail comment cet assouplissement rapide des cellules se produit. Ils pourraient aussi essayer d’utiliser cette idée pour construire de nouveaux types de robots ou d’appareils qui bougent rapidement et en douceur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que l’assouplissement rapide des cellules extérieures permet à la dionée de se refermer rapidement, révélant un nouveau processus de mouvement chez les plantes avec des applications possibles en robotique souple.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo las trampas de la Venus atrapamoscas se cierran tan rápido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Venus atrapamoscas es una planta carnívora famosa por cerrar sus trampas muy rápido para atrapar insectos. Los científicos han estudiado esta planta durante mucho tiempo para entender cómo se mueve tan rápido cuando la mayoría de las plantas se mueven lentamente.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio reciente encontró que las células exteriores de la hoja de la planta se ablandan rápidamente después de que un insecto la toca. Este ablandamiento permite que la hoja se doble hacia adentro y cierre la trampa en menos de un segundo. Este descubrimiento fue publicado en la revista Science en junio de 2026.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos ven que las células de una planta se ablandan tan rápido. Entender este proceso ayuda a aprender más sobre cómo funcionan las plantas. También podría inspirar a los ingenieros a crear robots blandos que cambien de forma rápidamente al cambiar la rigidez de sus materiales.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente estudiarán cómo ocurre este rápido ablandamiento celular en detalle. También podrían intentar usar esta idea para construir nuevos tipos de robots o dispositivos que se muevan rápido y suavemente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los científicos descubrieron que el rápido ablandamiento de las células exteriores permite que la Venus atrapamoscas cierre sus trampas rápidamente, revelando un nuevo proceso de movimiento en plantas con posibles aplicaciones en la robótica blanda.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a Venus flytrap in mid-snap, its two curved lobes elegantly flipping inward with delicate softness, surrounded by subtle glowing cells on the outer leaf surface symbolizing rapid cell softening, set against a simple natural background with muted greens and browns.

articles.title: Toy universe shows that time could be a quantum illusion
articles.summary: An experiment with a toy universe made up of extremely cold atoms shows how time can emerge from quantum interactions, instead of existing by default
articles.content:
An experiment with a toy universe made up of extremely cold atoms shows how time can emerge from quantum interactions, instead of existing by default

Does time actually exist? Bruce Rolff/Stocktrek Images/Getty Images

The passage of time may be nothing more than an illusion that emerges from the quantum interactions between different parts of the universe – at least, this is true in a toy model of the cosmos. The experiment may offer hints as to the nature of time in our own universe.

Giovanni Barontini at the University of Birmingham, UK, started thinking about time while watching his 6-year-old son play. “He was building his own small universe, and I was thinking that that’s pretty much what we do also in our labs, when we build an ultracold-atoms system,” he says. “But then I was starting to think that this is also quite boring as a universe, because there’s not much going on there, and if nothing happens, it’s like if time is not passing by.”

To investigate whether time is truly an illusion in such systems, Barontini used lasers and electromagnetic forces to cool around 20,000 rubidium atoms to temperatures close to absolute zero. He divided the atoms of this toy universe into two sectors, one labelled “bright” and the other “dark”, in analogy to dark matter.

This initial universe was essentially timeless and unchanging, but then Barontini used lasers to coax the two sectors into exchanging atoms and thus interacting on a quantum level. This changed the entropy, or disorder, of the universe, and we know that in our universe, time flows in the direction of increasing entropy. Consequently, Barontini could define an internal time for the toy universe. What’s more, he could use this new time in the Schrödinger equation, which describes how quantum systems evolve, to calculate the atoms’ quantum states, finding that it matched with the results of the experiment.

There is precedent for this view of time as something that arises from quantum correlations or interactions, rather than being a given: in atomic physics, the idea was first floated by physicist Nevill Mott in the 1930s and has been explored theoretically ever since. It wasn’t until 2013 that Marco Genovese at the National Metrology Institute of Italy and his colleagues offered the first proof of its feasibility in an experiment with entangled particles of light. Here, too, a sense of time came from quantum correlations.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

“The present work further elaborates on this idea with some significant progress,” says Genovese. In particular, the cold-atom universe is more complex than the one made using light, and Barontini was able to make the Schrödinger equation work with the system’s internal time, which hadn’t been done before.

Claus Kiefer at the University of Cologne, Germany, says that this toy universe experiment connects to the larger problem of how to combine gravity and quantum theory into a single framework that could apply to our universe at all scales. This question remains open, but some physicists have suggested that such a theory would be marked by the absence of time at the most fundamental level, he says. The new experiment mimics this situation, but Kiefer says that there are also differences – for instance, as ultracold atoms move between sectors, they don’t interact in complex ways that are expected in a bigger universe.

But Carlo Rovelli at Aix-Marseille University in France says that these types of experiments can’t discover something new about time because they are constructed based on physics that we already understand. Yet having them as mimics of big open problems might provide inspiration on how to treat unknown physics just like the infamously elusive issue of quantum gravity, he says.

For Barontini, the new study is an experimental confirmation of ideas that have been around for a long time and, as such, a demonstration that they aren’t fully out of contention. But it isn’t a confirmation that this is how time actually works at all scales, he says.

Cosmologists, who study the whole universe rather than lab-made toy models, are likely to have objections to the work, says Barontini. Yet he wants to explore the ultracold miniverse further, for instance by using lasers to create regions that atoms cannot move away from, similar to the pull of a black hole.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant experimental breakthrough in quantum physics, demonstrating how time can emerge from quantum interactions in a toy universe made of ultracold atoms. This experiment advances understanding of fundamental physics concepts with broad implications for quantum theory and cosmology, offering meaningful scientific progress and inspiration.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create toy universe showing time can emerge from quantum interactions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered if time is something that exists on its own or if it comes from deeper physical processes. Some theories suggest time might be an illusion that appears because of how parts of the universe interact on a tiny, quantum level.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Birmingham created a tiny model universe using about 20,000 rubidium atoms cooled to nearly absolute zero. They split these atoms into two groups, called "bright" and "dark," and used lasers to make them interact. These interactions changed the disorder (or entropy) in the system, allowing the scientists to define a kind of "internal time" that matched predictions from quantum physics equations.

**Impact** – This experiment is important because it shows for the first time in a complex system how time can come from quantum interactions instead of being a basic feature of the universe. It builds on earlier work with particles of light but uses atoms, which are more complex. The findings help scientists explore big questions about how time and the laws of physics fit together, especially when combining quantum mechanics with gravity.

**What's next step** – The lead researcher wants to continue studying this cold atom universe by creating areas where atoms are trapped, similar to black holes, to see how time and quantum effects behave in even more complicated setups. This work could inspire new ways to understand the universe at its most fundamental level.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new experiment with ultracold atoms shows that time can emerge from quantum interactions, offering fresh clues about the nature of time in the universe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent un univers miniature montrant que le temps peut émerger des interactions quantiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps si le temps existe en soi ou s’il résulte de processus physiques plus profonds. Certaines théories suggèrent que le temps pourrait être une illusion qui apparaît grâce aux interactions entre différentes parties de l’univers au niveau quantique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Birmingham ont créé un petit univers modèle avec environ 20 000 atomes de rubidium refroidis presque au zéro absolu. Ils ont divisé ces atomes en deux groupes, appelés « brillant » et « sombre », et ont utilisé des lasers pour les faire interagir. Ces interactions ont modifié le désordre (ou entropie) du système, ce qui a permis aux scientifiques de définir un « temps interne » correspondant aux prédictions des équations de la physique quantique.

**Impact** – Cette expérience est importante car elle montre pour la première fois dans un système complexe que le temps peut venir des interactions quantiques, plutôt que d’être une caractéristique de base de l’univers. Elle s’appuie sur des travaux précédents avec des particules de lumière, mais utilise des atomes, qui sont plus complexes. Ces résultats aident les scientifiques à explorer de grandes questions sur la nature du temps et des lois physiques, notamment en combinant la mécanique quantique avec la gravité.

**Prochaine étape** – Le chercheur principal souhaite continuer à étudier cet univers d’atomes froids en créant des zones où les atomes sont piégés, un peu comme des trous noirs, pour observer comment le temps et les effets quantiques se comportent dans des configurations plus complexes. Ce travail pourrait inspirer de nouvelles façons de comprendre l’univers à son niveau le plus fondamental.

**Phrase clé** – Une nouvelle expérience avec des atomes ultrafroids montre que le temps peut émerger des interactions quantiques, offrant de nouveaux indices sur la nature du temps dans l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean un universo de juguete que muestra que el tiempo puede surgir de interacciones cuánticas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo si el tiempo existe por sí mismo o si proviene de procesos físicos más profundos. Algunas teorías sugieren que el tiempo podría ser una ilusión que aparece por cómo las partes del universo interactúan a nivel cuántico.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Birmingham crearon un pequeño modelo de universo con unos 20,000 átomos de rubidio enfriados casi al cero absoluto. Dividieron estos átomos en dos grupos llamados "brillante" y "oscuro" y usaron láseres para hacer que interactuaran. Estas interacciones cambiaron el desorden (o entropía) del sistema, lo que permitió a los científicos definir un "tiempo interno" que coincidió con las predicciones de las ecuaciones de la física cuántica.

**Impacto** – Este experimento es importante porque muestra por primera vez en un sistema complejo cómo el tiempo puede surgir de las interacciones cuánticas en lugar de ser una característica básica del universo. Se basa en trabajos anteriores con partículas de luz, pero usa átomos, que son más complejos. Los resultados ayudan a los científicos a explorar grandes preguntas sobre cómo el tiempo y las leyes de la física se unen, especialmente al combinar la mecánica cuántica con la gravedad.

**Próximo paso** – El investigador principal quiere seguir estudiando este universo de átomos ultrafríos creando áreas donde los átomos queden atrapados, similar a los agujeros negros, para ver cómo se comportan el tiempo y los efectos cuánticos en configuraciones más complejas. Este trabajo podría inspirar nuevas formas de entender el universo a nivel fundamental.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo experimento con átomos ultrafríos muestra que el tiempo puede surgir de interacciones cuánticas, ofreciendo nuevas pistas sobre la naturaleza del tiempo en el universo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two distinct glowing atom clusters—one bright and one dark—connected by delicate, shimmering quantum threads in an abstract cosmic space, symbolizing their quantum interactions and the emergence of time within a cold, simplified universe.
